draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to encouraging, celebratory language than neutral weather reporting
facade.label: Motivational tone
facade.acceptImplies: Voice should celebrate effort and conditions; use energetic language like 'Perfect tempo conditions!' or 'You've got this weather'
facade.rejectImplies: Voice should stay neutral and factual; focus on data delivery like 'Temperature 52°F, Wind 8mph Northwest'

facade.hypothesis: Runners want visibility and clarity over visual warmth; a bright, high-contrast interface (neon accent on dark) signals performance-focused rather than cozy
facade.label: Glowing route map
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward energetic, tech-forward mood—crisp feedback, strong contrast, digital-first aesthetic
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers softer, more organic mood—muted palette, gentler feedback, nature-inspired or analog-feeling design

evidence.hypothesis: Runners respond better to encouraging, celebratory language than neutral weather reporting
evidence.implication: Voice should celebrate effort and conditions; use energetic language like 'Perfect tempo conditions!' or 'You've got this weather'

facade.hypothesis: Runner apps split between high-visibility energy colors and calm, minimal palettes. The app's color temperature will signal whether it prioritizes motivation or focus during a run.
facade.label: Bright neon vs muted earth
facade.acceptImplies: User resonates with vibrant, energetic, high-contrast color schemes—likely wants the app to feel motivating and performance-focused.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers subdued, natural, or dark color palettes—likely wants the app to feel calm, non-distracting, and comfortable for early morning or evening runs.

facade.hypothesis: Runners prioritize quick glance data (temp, pace, route) over full-screen information architecture; a persistent sidebar preserves context while cards in the main area allow scannable, modular content.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure (sidebar vs. horizontal tabs) and content grouping (cards vs. continuous scroll)
facade.label: Sidebar nav with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Runners want persistent access to key metrics and prefer modular, scannable layouts that don't require full-page navigation.
facade.rejectImplies: Runners may prefer immersive, full-screen focus on a single metric at a time, or horizontal tab navigation that maximizes content area for run-in-progress scenarios.

facade.hypothesis: Runners want prominent metrics (pace, distance, time) as primary visual hierarchy, with contextual details (temperature, humidity, wind) de-emphasized or tucked away
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — what gets visual weight
facade.label: Big numbers, small text
facade.acceptImplies: User prioritizes glanceable performance stats; weather should support the run, not dominate it. Design favors large, scannable numbers with minimal prose.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants integrated narrative or balanced emphasis; weather story (conditions, alerts, advisory tone) matters as much as raw numbers. Design favors prose, cards, or mixed hierarchy.

facade.hypothesis: Runners want instant feedback on their current speed, so showing heart rate or pace as a number that updates with each step—triggered by touch—creates a real-time behavior loop that rewards checking the app mid-run.
facade.axisTargeted: Gesture-driven interaction vs passive display—does motion come FROM the runner's action or TO them automatically?
facade.label: Pace pulse on tap
facade.acceptImplies: Users expect the app to respond to deliberate touches (tap for splits, swipe for next metric) rather than auto-animate; behavior is reward-driven.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer passive, always-visible metrics that don't demand interaction; animation should flow without prompting.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual weather reporting
Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere while planning runs
Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV index)
Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback when checking conditions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual weather reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere while planning runs
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV index)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback when checking conditions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer personality-driven voice (humor, casual language) over motivational intensity; lighter tone reduces pressure and increases daily engagement
facade.label: Casual, playful tone
facade.acceptImplies: Design voice as conversational friend ('Nice easy day for a run') rather than hype coach; use humor and understatement
facade.rejectImplies: Runners want motivational push over casual banter; voice should stay encouraging and goal-focused, not comedic or dismissive of effort

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
